does: This paper introduces affective safety as a distinct and unified category of AI safety concerns, addressing harms that arise from large language models' engagement with human emotional and affective life. The authors develop a structured taxonomy identifying three recurring types of affective harms: affective self-alienation (where users become disconnected from their own emotional understanding through AI interaction), fairness and bias harms (where emotional content is treated inequitably across demographic groups), and relational harms (where AI systems undermine meaningful human relationships). Through systematic analysis across different LLM applications and contexts, they demonstrate that these harm types recur due to structural properties inherent in how AI systems process and respond to emotional content, rather than isolated incidents.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of AI ethics research that has traditionally prioritized epistemic harms (misinformation, hallucinations) and physical safety concerns while largely neglecting the emotional dimension. It extends psychological literature on human affective development and well-being alongside fairness frameworks in ML that document how systems can perpetuate biases. The paper responds to fragmented safety discussions where emotional concerns appear ad-hoc in content moderation policies or chatbot guidelines, drawing connections to emerging work on AI's social impacts and relational autonomy in philosophy. It synthesizes insights from human-computer interaction research on parasocial relationships and emotional labor while grounding these in concrete LLM safety failures that existing frameworks fail to adequately capture.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing dedicated affective safety evaluation metrics and red-teaming methodologies sensitive to cumulative emotional harms rather than single-instance risks. Future work can build technical detection methods for affective self-alienation and relational degradation, potentially using psychological assessment tools adapted for AI interaction contexts. It points toward regulatory approaches that recognize emotional well-being alongside traditional safety categories, enabling policy design for systems deployed in therapeutic or intimate contexts. The taxonomy supports research into how LLM training objectives might be modified to better respect human emotional autonomy, and enables cross-disciplinary collaboration between AI safety researchers, psychologists, and philosophers to develop more holistic safety standards that treat emotional life as central rather than peripheral to responsible AI deployment.
